title_en: Nikah and Family Law — Maliki Positions
title_ar: النكاح وأحكام الأسرة في المذهب المالكي
content_en:
The family law section of Hashiyat al-Dusuqi covers marriage, divorce, and personal status in the Maliki school with the detail that has made this work the primary reference for Maliki family law judges across North and West Africa, where the school has been dominant for centuries.

On marriage, the Maliki school requires the wali (guardian) and two witnesses as conditions of a valid marriage contract. A distinctive Maliki feature is the doctrine of kafaa (compatibility): while all schools recognize kafaa as desirable, the Maliki school gives it particular weight — the wali has the right to object to a marriage on grounds of lack of social compatibility, and in some formulations this right of objection belongs to the tribal or family group ('asabiyyah) as a whole. Al-Dusuqi explains these rules and their boundaries, distinguishing the legitimate role of family input from the prohibited coercion of the woman into an unwanted marriage.

The Maliki school's position on secret marriage (zawaj al-sirr) is noteworthy: a marriage conducted without publicity or the intention to publicize is treated as void in the Maliki school even if all technical conditions are met, because public announcement (i'lan al-nikah) is treated as a condition of validity, not merely a recommendation. This position — based on the prophetic instruction to publicize marriages and beat the tambourine — differs from the majority of scholars who treat publicity as Sunnah but not a condition of validity.

The divorce sections cover talaq, khul', fasakh (judicial annulment), and the specific conditions for each. The Maliki school's recognition of judicial dissolution of marriage on grounds of harm (darar) is particularly developed, providing an important avenue for women in difficult marriages. The Maliki judge may dissolve a marriage if the husband causes established harm to the wife even if he does not pronounce talaq. Al-Dusuqi also covers the Maliki rules on ila' and zihar — forms of improper oaths relating to marriage — and the rulings on custody and maintenance during and after the marital relationship.
